trajectory.description: Observation: Starting from scratch (iteration 0). Need to build a complete training/inference pipeline for DomainNet-345 → Quickdraw DG. Decision: Start with DINOv2 ViT-B/14 because it is highlighted as a strong SSL backbone for OOD features and directly comparable to ERM++ ablations. Use 4-GPU DDP, strong augmentations (RandAugment, RandomGrayscale), domain-balanced sampling, Mixup+CutMix, AdamW+cosine schedule with warmup, AMP, and EMA. First run: only 2 epochs to validate the pipeline, measure iteration time, and submit a baseline eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: The previous training run failed after ~22 seconds. The stderr only shows PyTorch elastic's generic 'all ranks exited with code 1' wrapper, hiding the actual root cause. Analysis: I need to surface the real Python traceback before attempting DDP again. The failure is likely an import error, model instantiation error, or missing cached weights for DINOv2. Decision: Run a minimal single-GPU debug script that imports the model, loads one sample, and does a forward pass. This will reveal the true error without DDP noise.

Observation: The debug script confirmed model instantiation and dataset loading work, but revealed a collate error when no transform is applied (expected). The real training failure in iter 1 was likely a DDP/sync issue in the original train.py. Analysis: Need a clean, robust train.py that handles DDP correctly, EMA with DDP, domain-balanced sampling per rank, AMP, and logging to metrics.jsonl without spamming stdout. Decision: Rewrite train.py from scratch with careful DDP setup, barrier after validation, and EMA that saves a full state_dict. Run 2 epochs on 4 GPUs to validate the pipeline and submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iterations 1 and 3 failed with DDP timeout (rendezvous/client socket) after ~10 min; iteration 2 single-GPU debug worked. Analysis: The timeout likely comes from torchrun hostname resolution issues in the container, or from a subtle code crash causing one rank to exit while others hang. To validate the end-to-end pipeline and get a baseline score as fast as possible, I will first run a lightweight ResNet-50 backbone for only 2 epochs on 4 GPUs. I rewrote train.py from scratch with robust DDP setup, explicit localhost binding (MASTER_ADDR=127.0.0.1), proper DistributedSampler, EMA, and metrics logging. Predict.py now auto-detects the backbone from the checkpoint and handles module. prefixes. Decision: Execute the minimal ResNet-50 run to measure iteration time, verify DDP, and submit eval immediately. Once the pipeline is proven, I will switch to DINOv2 and scale epochs.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 4 successfully trained ResNet-50 for 2 epochs on 4 GPUs and submitted eval, achieving 1.04% test accuracy on Quickdraw. The pipeline works end-to-end. However, the checkpoint saved the EMA model even though it performed far worse than the regular model on source validation (5% vs 64%), so predict.py loaded the weaker model. Analysis: EMA with decay=0.9999 barely updates from pretrained weights in 2 epochs. Also, ResNet-50 is not the strongest backbone for this extreme domain gap; DINOv2 ViT-B/14 is highlighted as a strong SSL backbone for OOD features. Decision: Switch to DINOv2, train for 5 epochs to validate DDP with this backbone, fix checkpoint saving to always store the best-performing weights (regular or EMA) under the 'model' key, enable moderate Mixup/CutMix, increase RandomGrayscale probability to 0.5 to simulate the Quickdraw domain, use gradient clipping for stable ViT fine-tuning, and use a faster EMA decay (0.999). After training, submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 5 crashed immediately after training step 0 with DINOv2 on 4 GPUs. The torchrun wrapper hid the traceback, but all ranks exited simultaneously with code 1. This pattern strongly suggests a DataLoader worker crash (segfault/OOM in forked child) rather than a Python exception in the main loop, because step 0 completed successfully (loss printed) and the crash occurred before step 1. CUDA context + forked DataLoader workers is a classic cause, especially after loading transformers-backed models. Analysis: Mixup might also be a factor, but the priority is to eliminate the hidden crash source. I will rewrite train.py to remove Mixup for now, set num_workers=0 (no forked workers), keep all other robust features (AdamW, cosine+warmup, gradient clipping, EMA with faster decay 0.999, strong augmentations including RandAugment and RandomGrayscale p=0.5). This gives a clean DINOv2 baseline. Decision: Write the simplified, worker-free train.py, run 2 epochs on 4 GPUs with explicit localhost binding, and immediately submit eval to validate the DINOv2 pipeline and measure Quickdraw baseline accuracy.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iterations 5 and 6 crashed immediately after training step 0 with DINOv2 on 4 GPUs (rank 2 exits with code 1, others get SIGTERM). Single-GPU debug works. Analysis: DINOv2 from transformers includes a pooler layer whose output is computed but unused in the original forward pass; in DDP with find_unused_parameters=False, parameters receiving no gradients cause a hard crash. Decision: Fix model.py to consume pooler_output when available (ensuring the pooler gets gradients), and set find_unused_parameters=True in DDP for dinov2 as a safety net. Rewrite train.py with correct checkpoint saving (save whichever of regular/EMA is best under the 'model' key), keep strong augmentations (RandAugment + RandomGrayscale p=0.5), AMP, AdamW+cosine+warmup, gradient clipping, and EMA. Run 2 epochs to validate the DINOv2+DDP pipeline and immediately submit eval.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 7 successfully trained DINOv2 for 2 epochs on 4 GPUs without DDP crashes. Best source-domain validation accuracy was 0.5293 (EMA after epoch 0). The checkpoint checkpoints/best.pt was saved. We still need the actual Quickdraw test accuracy to establish the DINOv2 baseline and decide how to scale training. Analysis: The pipeline is now stable. Submitting eval is the highest-priority next step to measure iteration time end-to-end and get a target-domain score. Decision: Submit the current best checkpoint for evaluation immediately. Based on the returned accuracy and remaining budget, I will then scale epochs and tune hyperparameters.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 8 eval failed with a shape mismatch in backbone.embeddings.position_embeddings: checkpoint has [1, 1370, 768] (518x518, patch 14) but predict.py's Dinov2Config() instantiated [1, 257, 768] (224x224). Analysis: DINOv2-base pretrained weights store 518x518 position embeddings. During training, AutoModel.from_pretrained loaded them fine. In predict.py we use pretrained=False, and the default Dinov2Config() creates a smaller embedding table. Decision: Fix model.py to explicitly set image_size=518 and patch_size=14 in Dinov2Config so the architecture is identical whether pretrained=True or False. Run a quick local debug load to verify the existing checkpoint loads, then immediately submit eval to get the DINOv2 Quickdraw baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 9 debug script confirmed the fixed DINOv2 model loads the checkpoint without shape mismatch and produces correct output shape. Analysis: The eval failure in iter 8 was solely due to Dinov2Config defaulting to 224x224 when pretrained=False. The explicit image_size=518/patch_size=14 fix resolves this. Decision: Submit the existing best checkpoint for evaluation immediately to obtain the DINOv2 Quickdraw baseline and measure end-to-end eval time. This unblocks scaling decisions for the remaining ~9.5h and 21 iterations.

trajectory.description: Observation: DINOv2 trained for 2 epochs achieved 4.71% Quickdraw accuracy (vs 1.04% for ResNet-50), confirming both backbone superiority and end-to-end pipeline stability. Analysis: 2 epochs is severe underfitting; published strong results require much longer fine-tuning. Additionally, the training domains are imbalanced (real ≈42% of data), which likely hurts generalization to the sketch-like Quickdraw target. Decision: (1) Scale training to 15 epochs, (2) add domain-balanced sampling using domain_labels.txt to upweight sketch/clipart domains, (3) re-enable Mixup (alpha=0.2) now that DataLoader workers are stable, (4) add horizontal-flip TTA in predict.py, and (5) keep AdamW+cosine LR, AMP, gradient clipping, and EMA. This should substantially lift accuracy while staying well within the ~9.5h budget.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 11 completed a 15-epoch DINOv2 training run (~102 min) with domain-balanced sampling, Mixup, and EMA. Source-domain validation accuracy reached 30.8%. The last submitted eval (2 epochs) was 4.71% Quickdraw accuracy. Analysis: I need to measure how much the 15-epoch run improved target-domain generalization before deciding whether to scale further or tune hyperparameters. Decision: Write an updated predict.py that handles batched inference and optional horizontal-flip TTA, then immediately submit the current best checkpoint for evaluation.

trajectory.description: Observation: The 15-epoch DINOv2 run (iter 11) achieved only 2.31% Quickdraw accuracy, significantly worse than the 2-epoch run's 4.71%. Analysis: Full fine-tuning at lr=1e-3 caused catastrophic forgetting — source val accuracy dropped from 52.9% (pretrained/EMA-epoch-0) to 30.8%, and target accuracy collapsed. The pretrained backbone features are clearly superior for OOD generalization; aggressive fine-tuning destroys them. Decision: Freeze the DINOv2 backbone entirely and train only the classification head for 10 epochs with a high head LR (1e-2). This preserves the SSL-pretrained features while learning a better head than the 2-epoch run. I will also make Mixup default-off and disable domain-balanced sampling to isolate variables. If this improves over 4.71%, I can then try careful partial unfreezing.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 13 crashed after 17 seconds in DDP when trying to freeze the DINOv2 backbone. The pattern (rank 0 exits immediately, others get SIGTERM) suggests DDP incompatibility with frozen parameters or a setup bug. Analysis: DDP has been consistently problematic in this environment, while single-GPU training is much simpler and stable. Given frozen-backbone training (only ~265K head params trainable), a single GPU with batch size 256 is efficient enough (~10 min/epoch). The 15-epoch full fine-tuning got 2.31% (worse than 2-epoch's 4.71%), confirming catastrophic forgetting. Strategy: freeze backbone entirely, train only the classification head for 10 epochs with high LR (1e-2), domain-balanced sampling, and strong augmentations. This preserves SSL features while learning a domain-robust head. Decision: Write a robust single-GPU train.py, dataset.py, keep model.py and predict.py compatible. Run 10 epochs and submit eval immediately after training.

trajectory.description: Observation: The best Quickdraw accuracy achieved so far was 4.71% with a 2-epoch full DINOv2 fine-tune (iteration 10). The 15-epoch DINOv2 run collapsed to 2.31% due to catastrophic forgetting, and the 10-epoch frozen-backbone run only reached 0.75%, confirming that (a) some backbone adaptation is crucial, but (b) aggressive tuning destroys OOD generalization. The old best checkpoint was overwritten, so I must train a new strong model. Analysis: I have not yet tried CLIP ViT-B/16, which is highlighted as the backbone used by CLIP-DG papers and may transfer better to sketch-like Quickdraw due to its diverse web-scale pretraining. Additionally, all prior runs used a simple Linear classification head; adding a small MLP head (hidden→GELU→Dropout→out) is a standard fine-tuning recipe for ViTs and often yields a few points of improvement. Decision: (1) Update model.py to use an MLP head for all ViT backbones (DINOv2, ViT-B/16 AugReg, CLIP) while keeping ResNet-50 unchanged. (2) Run a conservative 3-epoch full fine-tuning of CLIP ViT-B/16 with lr=1e-4, batch size 256, single GPU (stable), domain-balanced sampling, strong augmentations (RandAugment + RandomGrayscale p=0.5), and EMA. (3) Immediately submit the resulting checkpoint for evaluation to measure whether CLIP outperforms the old DINOv2 baseline.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iter 16 eval failed because predict.py tried to load the old DINOv2 checkpoint (checkpoints/best.pt, saved with a simple Linear head) into the current model.py which instantiates an MLP head for DINOv2. The keys mismatch (head.weight vs head.0.weight). Analysis: We also have checkpoints/clip_3ep.pt from the recent training run, but we never evaluated it. Before scaling training further, we must (a) make predict.py backward-compatible with both Linear and MLP checkpoints by auto-detecting head architecture from saved keys, and (b) submit clip_3ep.pt for evaluation to get the CLIP (or updated DINOv2) Quickdraw baseline. Decision: Rewrite model.py to accept a head_type argument ('linear' or 'mlp') and rewrite predict.py to infer head_type from checkpoint keys. Then run a quick inspection of clip_3ep.pt and submit it immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 17 eval returned 23.28% Quickdraw accuracy with CLIP ViT-B/16 (3 epochs, MLP head), which is a massive improvement over the prior 4.71% and exceeds published SOTA for this DG task. Analysis: The model is still underfitting source domains (76.5% val) and has only seen 3 epochs. Conservative continued fine-tuning should raise target accuracy further without catastrophic forgetting. Decision: Continue training from checkpoints/clip_3ep.pt for 5 additional epochs at a reduced LR (5e-5) to refine features safely. Update train.py to support --pretrained_checkpoint, keep single-GPU stability, EMA, domain-balanced sampling, and strong augmentations. Save to a new checkpoint name to preserve the existing best. After training, submit eval immediately.

trajectory.description: Observation: Iteration 18 successfully continued training CLIP ViT-B/16 for 5 additional epochs (total 8 epochs) at reduced LR 5e-5, starting from the 23.28% checkpoint. Best source validation accuracy improved to 79.25% (EMA). Training took ~68 minutes. We have ~1.48 hours (~89 minutes) and 12 iterations remaining. Analysis: The model is improving on source validation without catastrophic forgetting so far, but we must measure target-domain Quickdraw accuracy immediately to determine whether longer fine-tuning improves or degrades OOD generalization. Decision: Submit the newly trained checkpoints/clip_8ep.pt for evaluation right now to obtain the 8-epoch Quickdraw baseline. Based on the result and remaining time, I can then decide whether to train further, ensemble checkpoints, or stop.

trajectory.description: Observation: The 8-epoch CLIP continuation (21.3%) degraded target accuracy compared with the 3-epoch CLIP checkpoint (23.28%), indicating that further fine-tuning hurts OOD generalization. The 3-epoch result already exceeds published SOTA for this DG task. With only ~47 minutes left, starting a new training run is risky and may not beat 23.28%. Analysis: The highest-ROI action is to improve inference on the existing best checkpoint without additional training. Standard TenCrop TTA (Resize 256 → center + 4 corners, plus horizontal flips) often provides a small but reliable boost over single-view + flip TTA, and it requires only modifying predict.py. Decision: Rewrite predict.py to perform TenCrop TTA by default (batch size 320 crops), run a quick local smoke test on 10 images to verify no crash, and immediately submit the best checkpoint (clip_3ep.pt) for evaluation.
